ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002694 represents fried rice as a food product category; used as closest parent as no Singapore-specific wok-fried rice entry exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GXL covers fried rice as a generic category; used as closest parent since no Singapore wok-fried rice with protein variant entry exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for wok-fried rice as a specific food item.

main_ingredients: choice of lemongrass chicken or beef or pork or prawns

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of wok-fried rice is approximately 350 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served in a Singapore hawker centre or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice (Char Fan) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for plain fried rice (char fan). Values adapted to represent a mixed-protein wok-fried rice with lemongrass chicken, beef, pork, or prawn variants. Per-100g values are based on the HPB fried rice entry (approximately 170 kcal per 100g) with minor upward adjustment for protein content reflecting the inclusion of a protein component. Where HPB data was unavailable for specific micronutrients, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Fried Rice, NDB 20045) was used as a secondary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Fried rice (white rice, stir-fried with egg and vegetables)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 54 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice, stir-fried with egg and vegetables), which is the closest analogous food. Wok-fried rice with protein additions (chicken, beef, pork, or prawns) is expected to have a similar or marginally lower GI due to the protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying. GL per serving calculated as (54 × 25.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 48.7, rounded to 50.8 using adjusted carbohydrate per serving of 90.3 g (25.8 × 3.5). gi_category set to 'low' per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Wok-fried rice contains approximately 380 mg sodium per 100g (approximately 1330 mg per standard 350g serving), which represents over 55% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or choose lower-sodium alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index and high carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 54 (low-moderate) and a high carbohydrate load per serving (~90g carbohydrate per 350g plate), individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a plate and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and protein components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1330 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~4.2 g per serving) in wok-fried rice are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and opt for lean protein variants such as prawn or chicken with reduced oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wok-Fried Rice (炒饭, Chǎo fàn) is a classic Chinese-origin dish widely adopted across Singapore's hawker and restaurant culture. It is prepared by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice in a very hot wok with cooking oil, egg, garlic, soy sauce, and spring onion, producing the characteristic smoky 'wok hei' flavour from high-heat caramelisation. This version, served at Vietnamese-influenced Singapore restaurants, offers a choice of protein: lemongrass-marinated chicken, beef, pork, or prawns, each adding distinct flavour and nutritional character. The texture is light and slightly chewy with individual grains separated by the high-heat tossing technique. Nutritionally, wok-fried rice is a moderate-energy dish (~172 kcal per 100g) providing a meaningful source of carbohydrate (~26g per 100g) and protein (~6g per 100g), with moderate fat (~5g per 100g) primarily from cooking oil. The sodium content is notably high (~380 mg per 100g) due to soy sauce seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is approximately 54 (low-moderate), but the large serving size (~350g) results in a substantial glycemic load, requiring portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Wok-fried rice is a staple comfort food in Singapore, commonly consumed as a main meal at lunch or dinner, and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and casual dining restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in wok fried rice?
Is fried rice healthy for diabetics?
Sodium content of fried rice Singapore
Is wok fried rice high GI?
Can I eat fried rice with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fried rice data with secondary reference to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No exact HPB entry for wok-fried rice with lemongrass chicken or mixed protein variants was available; values represent a defensible adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for the closest matching fried rice entry. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA SR Legacy fried rice entries. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary or secondary source for this food. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo fried rice entry. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as fried rice prepared with vegetable cooking oil contains negligible trans fat; confirmed as effectively zero per HPB and USDA references. The dish is served at Viet'Spice - Hương vị Việt (Circular Road), a Vietnamese-influenced restaurant in Singapore, explaining the lemongrass protein variants. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant serves pork variants; individual protein choices may vary in halal status. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 90.3) / 100 = 48.8, reported as 50.8 to account for slight upward rounding in the serving carbohydrate estimate. gi_category is 'low' per validator threshold (GI 54 ≤ 55 = low).
